A persistent and strong subtropical ridge centered across the Southern Plains continued to provide Georgia with an unstable northwest flow aloft.  A series of upper-level disturbances were embedded within the flow, each generating rounds of thunderstorms across the Tennessee Valley which progressively moved southeast into Georgia.  This pattern remained in place through much of June and into early July, for that matter.  A quasi-stationary front extended westward from the Carolinas and Tennessee westward into Oklahoma.  Numerous convective outflow boundaries were noted throughout the Tennessee Valley and the Carolinas.  A complex of thunderstorms moved into north Georgia during the mid-afternoon and steadily propagated southward into central Georgia during the evening before diminishing.  Many reports of damaging wind and some hail were reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that three trees were down in the far southwestern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper short wave was moving from the central into the eastern U.S. around a subtropical ridge located over south Texas.  A cold front accompanied the short wave.  A warm, moist, unstable air mass continued across the southeastern U.S.  The combination of these features provided ample support for scattered to numerous thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  A number of these became strong producing penny-sized hail.  Isolated storms became severe producing quarter-sized hail and damaging downburst winds.  The most notable storm affected Jackson county in northeast Georgia where damaging downburst winds caused considerable damage to a large commercial building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whitfield County Emergency Management Director reported penny-sized hail near Rocky Face.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft was deepening across the eastern U.S. as trough deepened in the northeast U.S. and the ever present subtropical ridge amplified into the southern Plains.  The unstable northwest flow, combined with daytime heating, allowed for isolated to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorm development.  One storm briefly became severe in White county of northeast Georgia producing damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reports that at least 10 trees were down and several power poles were either down or bent over through the central part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong short waves were moving across the U.S.  These short waves were ejecting from a large upper trough in the western U.S.  These vigorous short waves encountered a hot, moist, unstable air mass across the U.S.  However, dry mid-level atmosphere air and west to west-northwest flow aloft promoted the development of organized lines of thunderstorms, which produced extensive strong outflow boundaries supporting wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph, even greater in some cases.  During the afternoon of the 18th, such thunderstorm gust fronts affected northwest Georgia in particular with widespread damaging wind gusts, likely in excess of 70 mph, blowing down hundreds of trees, dozens of power lines, and causing damage to some structures.  This area of thunderstorms progressed southeast toward Atlanta, before dissipating during the mid-evening.  Considerable wind damage was noted in the northern and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta from this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fannin County 911 Center reported that at least six trees and several power lines were down around Epworth in the western part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge remained across the Southern Plains and was beginning to expand slightly eastward into the Mid-South.  However, a weak upper trough lingered across central Georgia.  Numerous outflow boundaries remained across the area from overnight thunderstorms.  With a moist, unstable, hot atmosphere remaining in place, scattered thunderstorms were once again the rule.  However, most of the activity on this day was confined to west central, southwest, and the western parts of Middle Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Management Director reported that a home in west central Fulton county, just southeast of Sandtown, was struck and set on fire by lightning.  The structure sustained at least moderate damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across south Georgia.  A persistent strong subtropical ridge remained over the south central U.S. with an equally persistent weak upper trough lingering over the southeast U.S.  A hot, humid, air mass remained in place across central and south Georgia and was sufficient for isolated thunderstorms to develop across this portion of the state during the afternoon.  The activity moved west and southwest around the southeast side of the subtropical ridge.  Damaging winds were once again the main issue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laurens County Emergency Management Director reported that several trees and power lines were down across the southeast part of the county from near Scott on the Johnson county line southwest to Lowery.  Some of the downed power lines caused grass fires as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down in southern Berrien county.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge remained across the Southern Plains and was beginning to expand slightly eastward into the Mid-South.  However, a weak upper trough lingered across central Georgia.  Numerous outflow boundaries remained across the area from overnight thunderstorms.  With a moist, unstable, hot atmosphere remaining in place, scattered thunderstorms were once again the rule.  However, most of the activity on this day was confined to west central, southwest, and the western parts of Middle Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail hail in the far eastern part of the county near Big Springs and the Meriwether county line along Interstate-185 north of Exit Number 42.

A series of strong short waves were moving across the U.S.  These short waves were ejecting from a large upper trough in the western U.S.  These vigorous short waves encountered a hot, moist, unstable air mass across the U.S.  However, dry mid-level atmosphere air and west to west-northwest flow aloft promoted the development of organized lines of thunderstorms, which produced extensive strong outflow boundaries supporting wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph, even greater in some cases.  During the afternoon of the 18th, such thunderstorm gust fronts affected northwest Georgia in particular with widespread damaging wind gusts, likely in excess of 70 mph, blowing down hundreds of trees, dozens of power lines, and causing damage to some structures.  This area of thunderstorms progressed southeast toward Atlanta, before dissipating during the mid-evening.  Considerable wind damage was noted in the northern and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta from this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported penny-sized hail near Jones Mill and Colwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moved across southeast Georgia in the afternoon which ignited scattered severe storms. The sea breezes and outflows provided enough low level convergence and surface instability was high. Widespread wind damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Down burst winds blew down several trees along Antioch Church Road. The trees landed on a power substation. A large portion of the county was without power, and the road to the power station was closed until 2 am LST on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of northwest flow short waves continued to impact the area, supported by a persistent strong subtropical ridge over Texas and an upper trough in the northeast United States.  An overnight mesoscale convective system (MCS) across the western Tennessee Valley, supported by one of these short waves, moved into northern Alabama during the morning hours of the 17th, then southeast into west central Georgia during the early and mid-afternoon.  Although the system was in a diminishing state, it remained organized and intense enough to produce damaging winds across west and into central Georgia as it move southeast through the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twiggs County 911 Center reported that at least seven trees were down across the northern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough and a seasonably strong cold front was sweeping through the southeast U.S.  However, the air mass in place was considerably more stable than what was usually in place with the April systems and had been unable to fully recover from the system that brought the historical tornado outbreak to the area less than one week prior.  Nonetheless, dynamics were sufficiently strong to support isolated strong to severe thunderstorms, mostly in the form of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny to nickel-sized hail in the southwest and west central part of the county near East Lake and Decatur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent squall line of severe thunderstorms raced eastward across the entire southeastern U.S. from the morning of April 4 through the afternoon of April 5, 2011. In a matter of just 30 hours, this squall line brought widespread damaging winds to the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, southeast Alabama, southwest and south central Georgia, northwest Florida, and the Mid Atlantic States. This system was one of the most prolific severe weather producers to ever impact the nation with widespread straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large oak tree was blown down into the middle of a road.  A car ran into it and the driver was killed.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low was developing in the middle Tennessee valley and slowly moving toward the southeast U.S.   A cold front accompanied the system.  The air mass across the region was marginally unstable.  As afternoon temperatures warmed into the low and mid 80s across the region, the approaching dynamics and instability combined to generate scattered thunderstorms which moved into the state from Alabama.  A few of these became strong to severe producing large hail and damaging winds.  By far, the main issue with these storms was damaging winds.  The events were generally confined to areas north and west of a line from Columbus to Atlanta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County 911 Center reported that up to nine trees were blown down in the Rome area.  A few power lines were also down.  A street light in Rome was also damaged from downed tree.  The Rome News Tribune reported that a tree was down on a home just northeast of Armuchee.  Several power lines were also down in the northern part of the county as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous closed upper low was moving from the Midwest into the Ohio Valley during the period of June 21st through the 23rd.  A slow moving cold front accompanied this system and was draped from Oklahoma into Ohio.  High pressure aloft across north Florida was keeping a hot, humid, unstable air mass in place across Georgia.  A complex of thunderstorms moved into northwest Georgia during the late afternoon/early evening before dissipating.  As this activity moved into northwest Georgia, it began to decay and produced an area of strong, gusty outflow winds causing damage in several northwest Georgia counties.  A separate area of less organized, isolated thunderstorms in east central Georgia also produced a small area of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga County Emergency Management Director reported that at least six trees were down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, closed low continued to move grudgingly through the southeast United States.  An associated nearly occluded front was located from middle Tennessee to the central Alabama Gulf Coast.  A warm, unstable air mass continued across Georgia in advance of the front.  Earlier convection had pushed a pre-frontal trough into central Georgia.  This trough served as a focus for scattered afternoon and early evening thunderstorms across east central and middle Georgia.  Several of these became severe producing damaging wind gusts.  All hail reported with these thunderstorms was penny-size or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wilkinson County 911 Center reported that several trees were down between Nicklesville and Toomsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was across the forecast area. This boundary in addition to active sea breezes and a passing upper level short wave trough produced scattered severe storms in the afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-term drought conditions continued during the month of July 2011, however several convective days during the month produced locally heavy rainfall over portions of the fires. Stalling fronts and sea breezes aided in this heavy rainfall producing convection as well as a lingering plume of deep tropical moisture. Despite the locally heavy rainfall over portions of the fire, it was not enough to mitigate the slow but steady progression of the Honey Prairie wildfire in the swamp. One of the Honey Prairie Incident Meteorologists stated that it will take a tropical system to recharge the water table from the bottom up, which is the only thing that will truly end the threat from this ongoing fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sweat Farm Again Wildfire was about 3 miles SW of Waresboro in Ware County, Ga. On the 5th, the fire had burned about 19,169 acres and was 75 percent contained. On the 17th, the fire was 92 percent contained. The Incident Status Summary (ISS) issued on the 17th for the fire indicated that it had threatened about 200 residences, damaged 1, and destroyed 2. There were about 300 outbuildings threatened and 11 were destroyed.  The cost to mitigate the fire was about $3M per the ISS on the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent and strong subtropical ridge centered across the Southern Plains continued to provide Georgia with an unstable northwest flow aloft.  A series of upper-level disturbances were embedded within the flow, each generating rounds of thunderstorms across the Tennessee Valley which progressively moved southeast into Georgia.  This pattern remained in place through much of June and into early July, for that matter.  A quasi-stationary front extended westward from the Carolinas and Tennessee westward into Oklahoma.  Numerous convective outflow boundaries were noted throughout the Tennessee Valley and the Carolinas.  A complex of thunderstorms moved into north Georgia during the mid-afternoon and steadily propagated southward into central Georgia during the evening before diminishing.  Many reports of damaging wind and some hail were reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lumpkin County Emergency Management Director relayed reports of a brief period of quarter-sized hail just northwest of Dahlonega, with mostly nickel-sized hail on the north and northeast side of Dahlonega.  The public observed nickel-sized hail between Dahlonega and Garland in the southeast part of the county.  There was also one tree down on Longbranch Road at GA 115W.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, closed low continued to move grudgingly through the southeast United States.  An associated nearly occluded front was located from middle Tennessee to the central Alabama Gulf Coast.  A warm, unstable air mass continued across Georgia in advance of the front.  Earlier convection had pushed a pre-frontal trough into central Georgia.  This trough served as a focus for scattered afternoon and early evening thunderstorms across east central and middle Georgia.  Several of these became severe producing damaging wind gusts.  All hail reported with these thunderstorms was penny-size or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Middle Georgia Electric Membership Cooperative reported that several trees and power lines were down along Connor Road, just south of Cedar Creek in northeast Wilcox county and along Antioch Church Road, near Antioch Church in southeast Wilcox county.  The Wilcox County Emergency Management Director reported that at least two trees were down just south of Abbeville in far eastern Wilcox county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong short waves were moving across the U.S.  These short waves were ejecting from a large upper trough in the western U.S.  These vigorous short waves encountered a hot, moist, unstable air mass across the U.S.  However, dry mid-level atmosphere air and west to west-northwest flow aloft promoted the development of organized lines of thunderstorms, which produced extensive strong outflow boundaries supporting wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph, even greater in some cases.  During the afternoon of the 18th, such thunderstorm gust fronts affected northwest Georgia in particular with widespread damaging wind gusts, likely in excess of 70 mph, blowing down hundreds of trees, dozens of power lines, and causing damage to some structures.  This area of thunderstorms progressed southeast toward Atlanta, before dissipating during the mid-evening.  Considerable wind damage was noted in the northern and northwestern suburbs of Atlanta from this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported that multiple structure fires were responded to by fire crews on Zoey Lee Drive Southwest off Temple Johnson Road in Rosebud.  The extent of the damages was not provided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge remained anchored across the central and south central U.S., with Georgia once again on the eastern periphery of the upper ridge.  This continued to provide Georgia and the southeast U.S. with unstable northwest flow aloft.  A disturbance was rotating around the back side of a deep upper trough off the northeast U.S. coast, providing added impetus for convection.  These synoptic features combined with the warm, moist, unstable air mass in place to provide for scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms across north Georgia.  A few of the storms became marginally severe, producing nickel to quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Union County Emergency Management Director reported nickel-sized hail northeast of Blairsville.  WSR-88D radar data suggests that similar-sized hail likely continued as the storm moved southwest toward Gaddistown and the Fannin county border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper short wave was moving from the central into the eastern U.S. around a subtropical ridge located over south Texas.  A cold front accompanied the short wave.  A warm, moist, unstable air mass continued across the southeastern U.S.  The combination of these features provided ample support for scattered to numerous thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  A number of these became strong producing penny-sized hail.  Isolated storms became severe producing quarter-sized hail and damaging downburst winds.  The most notable storm affected Jackson county in northeast Georgia where damaging downburst winds caused considerable damage to a large commercial building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed dime-sized hail on the west side of Atlanta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent squall line of severe thunderstorms raced eastward across the entire southeastern U.S. from the morning of April 4 through the afternoon of April 5, 2011. In a matter of just 30 hours, this squall line brought widespread damaging winds to the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, southeast Alabama, southwest and south central Georgia, northwest Florida, and the Mid Atlantic States. This system was one of the most prolific severe weather producers to ever impact the nation with widespread straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable northwest flow aloft continued to strengthen across the region in response to a vigorous upper low moving through the Great Lakes and the gradual retrogression of the strong subtropical ridge into Texas.  This combined with the continued hot, humid air mass in place across the region and numerous outflow boundaries from overnight upstream thunderstorm activity in the Tennessee Valley supported scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms throughout north and central Georgia.  In fact, this ended up being one of the more significant convective days for the region for August 2011.  Several of the storms became strong to severe with damaging downburst winds and producing penny to nickel-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga County Emergency Management Director reported that at least six trees were down across the northern half of the county from just northeast of Cloudland and near Trion to just south of Summerville.  Trees were down on Old Holland Road, two locations on U.S. Highway 27, at the Elder Trailer Park and in the Gore Area, on Scoggins Street in Summerville, and Park Avenue in Trion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent squall line of severe thunderstorms raced eastward across the entire southeastern U.S. from the morning of April 4 through the afternoon of April 5, 2011. In a matter of just 30 hours, this squall line brought widespread damaging winds to the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, southeast Alabama, southwest and south central Georgia, northwest Florida, and the Mid Atlantic States. This system was one of the most prolific severe weather producers to ever impact the nation with widespread straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down with thousands of people without power.  Tift Area Academy closed for the day because of storm damage to the school.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Irene passed by to the east of the Georgia coastline on August 26, 2011, producing beach erosion, high surf and rip currents across portions of southeast Georgia coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguards reported at least 15 rip current related rescues through the day on Tybee Island, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The summertime pattern continued.  A quasi-stationary front remained across north Georgia and extended westward into the mid-south.  A strong subtropical ridge also remained across the south, anchored in north Texas.  The upper ridge had retrograded slightly from the previous day allowing slightly more active northwest flow to evolve across the southeastern states.  A hot, very moist, unstable air mass remained in place across Georgia.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed across west central Georgia during the late morning and early afternoon and propagated on outflow boundaries northward and eastward across much of north and east Georgia.  Although very few of these storms reached severe limits, frequent to excessive cloud-to-ground lightning was noted with many of these storms, as well as locally very heavy rain on the order of two to three inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jefferson County 911 Center reported that lightning struck a tree at the intersection of Georgia Highway 88 and Moore Road in Stapletons Crossroads.  The tree was set on fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid and upper level ridge produced large thickness values over the southeast. These thickness values supported afternoon high temperatures in the upper 90s to low 100s inland and mid 90s along the coast. These temperatures in combination with low to mid 70s dewpoints pooling along an afternoon seabreeze, produced dangerous heat indices over portions of southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heat index of 118 F was measured from the Georgia Automated Environmental Monitoring Network at Bamboo Farm Coastal Gardens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent squall line of severe thunderstorms raced eastward across the entire southeastern U.S. from the morning of April 4 through the afternoon of April 5, 2011. In a matter of just 30 hours, this squall line brought widespread damaging winds to the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, southeast Alabama, southwest and south central Georgia, northwest Florida, and the Mid Atlantic States. This system was one of the most prolific severe weather producers to ever impact the nation with widespread straight line wind damage.

An upper level disturbance moved through the area and a few thunderstorms developed across south central Georgia.  A few storms produced hail.  Most of the hail producing storms were located over southeastern Georgia and southern South Carolina, but one storm did produce nickel sized hail over Ben Hill county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant late summer upper short wave was diving southeast from the mid-Mississippi Valley toward the southeastern United States.  A convective outflow boundary from overnight convective activity was present across central Georgia.  With ample moisture and continued hot temperatures, scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed quickly during the early and mid-afternoon across central Georgia, roughly from La Grange, to Thomaston, to Augusta southward.  A few isolated stronger thunderstorms were also noted across northeast Georgia during the afternoon.  Several of these thunderstorms became strong to severe producing damaging downburst winds and nickel to quarter-sized hail.  This was probably the most significant severe convective day for the month of August within the WFO FFC county warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter observed quarter-sized hail between Hamilton and Waverly Hall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued to create dry surface fuels. Scattered storms produced lightning strikes, especially during the middle of the month, which ignited numerous more fires across the forecast area. In addition, thunderstorm wind gusts and outflow boundaries produced sporadic and frequent wind shifts which enabled some fires to spread quickly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Honey Prairie Wildfire complex had burned about 152,994 acres on June 1st and was about 80 percent contained. By the 19th of the month, the fire burned about 233,606 acres and was only 60 percent contained again. Then, by the 30th the fire had burned 286,698 acres and was about 70 percent contained. By the 30th, it was estimated that the fire cost to date was around $30 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable northwest flow aloft continued to strengthen across the region in response to a vigorous upper low moving through the Great Lakes and the gradual retrogression of the strong subtropical ridge into Texas.  This combined with the continued hot, humid air mass in place across the region and numerous outflow boundaries from overnight upstream thunderstorm activity in the Tennessee Valley supported scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms throughout north and central Georgia.  In fact, this ended up being one of the more significant convective days for the region for August 2011.  Several of the storms became strong to severe with damaging downburst winds and producing penny to nickel-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twiggs County 911 Center reported that at least six trees were down in the Jeffersonville vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front was edging southwestward through South Carolina and into northeast Georgia.  An upper trough was moving through the mid-Atlantic and northeastern United States, while a large upper ridge was developing in the central United States.  The combination of unseasonably warm temperatures in the 80s, the backdoor front, and the northeast U.S. upper trough, provided support for a mesoscale convective complex of thunderstorms across upstate South Carolina.  The tail end of these storms skirted across extreme northeast Georgia, affecting mainly Madison county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Madison County 911 Center reported that a tin roof was blown off a church on Shirley Road just south of the Franklin county line and the city of Royston.  Some two-by-fours were also torn off the same building.  Three trees were down on Shirley Road and Doug Drake Road.  One large pine tree fell on a home causing damage to its roof.  A couple of telephone lines were down along Shirley Road as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A typical summer pattern remained in place across the southern U.S.  A broad subtropical ridge, anchored across the Arklatex region, was in control of the weather across the southern states.  A west-to-east quasi-stationary frontal system stretched across north Georgia westward into the mid-south.  A hot, humid, unstable air mass remained across the region.  Numerous convective outflow boundaries were evident across the region from previous day and overnight thunderstorm activity in various areas throughout the region.  Thus, it was no surprise that isolated to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms were once again the rule.  One of these in Madison county of northeast Georgia briefly became severe and produced damaging wind gusts along with pea-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Madison County Emergency Management Director reported that heavy rain, on the order of two to three inches with isolated four inch totals, caused minor flooding of low-lying areas and several drainage ditches to overflow.  Some lawns were standing in water for a few hours after the thunderstorms moved out of the area.  Flooding was not deemed to be of the level meeting local flood or flash flood criteria.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down across the county, some onto power lines. One tree fell on a car on Brookhaven Circle in Eastanolee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of severe thunderstorms moved through the CSRA and produced wind damage and some hail.  Damage was mainly to trees, some of which fell on homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported trees and powerlines down in the Hepzibah area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of severe thunderstorms moved through the CSRA and produced wind damage and some hail.  Damage was mainly to trees, some of which fell on homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reoprted trees and powerlines down in the Augusta and surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of severe thunderstorms moved through the CSRA and produced wind damage and some hail.  Damage was mainly to trees, some of which fell on homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a gust of 60 mph south of Augusta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a sagging surface front across south Georgia and the sea breeze fronts over a very unstable low level air mass produced widespread severe storms across the forecast area during the afternoon and evening hours. A passing upper level short wave trough also aided convection which enhanced mid level lapse rates during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail lasted for 20 minutes and caused major damage to a residential garden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp and deep negatively tilted full-latitude upper trough was sweeping through the eastern half of the United States.  An unseasonably warm, moist air mass was present in advance of the front, as was the case with many events during the 2011 - 2012 winter season.  In addition, a strong low-level jet contributed highly to a high shear/high helicity environment and the development of rotating cells within a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) that tracked from west central to east central Georgia during the afternoon.  An EF2 tornado tracked from Lee county in east central Alabama into Harris county in west central Georgia.  In addition, there were several reports of quarter-sized hail and thunderstorm wind damage, primarily across central Georgia during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth County Emergency Management Director reported that a tree fell on a sport utility vehicle (SUV) killing a 51-year old male inside the vehicle.  The incident took place on Brookwood Drive near McGinnis Ferry Road in the Johns Creek portion of far southern Forsyth county.  The person inside the vehicle was killed instantly.  One other tree was down in the northern part of the county as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The northern end of a large MCS moved across northeast Georgia during the late afternoon and early evening hours. The atmosphere was very unstable over the region and the storms knocked down numerous trees over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were blown down along highway 17 in the Toccoa area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lack of widespread persistent rainfall enabled the Honey Prairie Wildfire to resurrect itself across western portions of the Okefenokee Swamp. Portions affected included SE Clinch county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Honey Prairie wildfire had burned about 307,321 acres as of Sept. 13th. The fire was about 76 percent contained. On the 15th, the fire had burned about 308,780 acres. The fire has been burning since April 28, 2011.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was across southeast Georgia. Diurnal instability and a moist airmass fueled scattered storms across northeast Florida during the afternoon. A few severe storms developed and produced wind damage...one of which was across Charlton county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported trees were blown down across his property in Folkston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist yet weak south to southwest steering flow and active sea breezes supported heavy rainfall in showers and storms. Diurnal instability enhanced storms in the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall produced minor street flooding of low lying areas. Standing water up to 3 inches deep was reported along State Road 40 and U.S. Highway 17 in Kingsland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong near 600 dm subtropical ridge remained across Texas and the southern Plains.  Unstable northwest flow aloft along the eastern periphery of this upper high continued across the Mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A quasi-stationary front was located from west to east across north Georgia.  A disturbance in the northwest flow aloft was approaching and would push the front further south in the coming days.  These features combined with a hot, unstable air mass to support isolated strong to severe thunderstorm development across mainly north Georgia.  Thunderstorms developed and collapsed quickly, yet the environment on this day was particularly conducive to wet microbursts.  Widespread downed trees were noted in Fayette, Gwinnett, Henry, and Barrow counties from brief, yet intense thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta-Journal Constitution reported that at least 10 trees were blown down along Buford Highway in northern Gwinnett county from a thunderstorm downburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed during the evening hours as a short wave approached from the Southeast and interacted with an inland moving seabreeze over southeast Georgia. Thunderstorms became strong with damaging wind gusts while they developed within an atmosphere featuring DCAPE between 1000-1200 J/KG.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Liberty County 911 center reported one tree down on Pocohontas Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of a cold front and strong short wave trough in a moderately unstable air mass, resulted in the development of scattered to numerous thunderstorms across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Garden City Fire Department reported a tree and several large tree limbs down on power lines around Garden City, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front in combination with a weakening upper level shortwave resulted in scattered thunderstorm development across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported a tree down and blocking West Jones Avenue in Statesboro, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms moved through the CSRA and produced some isolated severe weather...mainly trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter size hail south of Augusta on Tobacco road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms moved through the CSRA and produced some isolated severe weather...mainly trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported trees down on Tobacco road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture, an unseasonably warm air mass, the right rear quadrant of an upper level jet, along with strong shear in place, allowed for an organized pre-frontal squall line to impact southeast Georgia and southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Trained Spotter reported 1 inch hail lasting 3 minutes in Hopeulikit.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jenkins County Warning Point reported 1 tree down along West Old Savannah Road, 3 miles southeast of Herndon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong late summer cold front was moving toward the southeast United States supported by strong northwest flow aloft on the east side of an ever persistent strong subtropical ridge across Texas and the southern plains.  With an unstable, hot surface environment in place across Georgia, the stage was set for scattered thunderstorms.  Activity was a little more widespread than on previous days.  The storms exhibited frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  A few became strong to severe producing penny to nickel-sized hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed nickel-sized hail between Villa Rica and Winston in the northwest part of the county and the Douglas County 911 Center relayed a report of penny-sized hail just east-southeast of Fairplay.  WSR-88D radar data suggested that similar size hail likely fell from northwest to southeast across much of the county.  The Douglas County 911 Center also reported that one tree was blown down just east-southeast of Fairplay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initially fired along subtle inland boundaries, then occasionally pulsed to severe levels as they interacted with a sea breeze and numerous complex outflow boundaries from other thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initially fired along subtle inland boundaries, then occasionally pulsed to severe levels as they interacted with a sea breeze and numerous complex outflow boundaries from other thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported dime to nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge remained in place across the south central U.S.  The upper ridge extended eastward into Alabama.  Meanwhile...an equally persistent elongated upper trough remained along the east coast, extending southward into the southeast U.S.  The air mass across Georgia remain hot and very moist and hence very unstable.  Subtle short waves in the northerly flow aloft aided in the development of isolated to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms once again.  A few of these became strong to severe producing damaging downburst winds.  No hail was observed with the events on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lamar County 911 Center reported that several trees were down across the far northeast and north central part of the county from Chappel Mill to just south of Orchard Hill along the Spalding/Lamar county line.  Some roads were briefly blocked by downed trees, including one lane of Interstate-75 in the far northeast corner of the county.  Scattered power outages were noted throughout the county, including within the Barnesville area.  At least one power line was down in the northeast part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From 3 to 5 inches of rain fell over parts of the south and east facing slopes of the southern Appalachian Mountains during the day on the 28th and early morning of the 29th. This caused a few areas of flash flooding in the mountains of northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Greenwood Dr and Lamb Rd were flooded on the north side of Dillard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong subtropical upper ridge over the southern plains remained in place.  However, the eastern extremity of the upper ridge across the mid-south was beginning to weaken as a short wave moved southeast through the Ohio and into the Eastern Tennessee Valley.  A moist, hot, unstable air mass remained in place across the southeast U.S.  The more favorable synoptic environment allowed for increased coverage of diurnal showers and thunderstorm activity.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed form most of these storms.  Isolated storms in west central and central Georgia became severe and produced quarter to half-dollar-sized hail during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed quarter-sized hail near Arkwright in the extreme northeast part of the county, including along Interstate-75 at the Arkwright Road Exit and Candlewood Suites.  Strong gusty winds were also observed in the area, which blew some light-weight objects about.  Wind gusts were estimated to be around 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shear axis extended southward across East-Central Georgia during the late evening into early evening hours. A weak disturbance tracked along the base of the shear axis into Southeast Georgia where surface instability was enhanced, thus leading to strong thunderstorm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down on Highway 251.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms with some of them being severe produced damaging winds and hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface heating and high precipital water values led to an unstable atmosphere during afternoon hours. A few thunderstorms developed within this environment along a lee trough and became strong as they encountered an inland moving seabreeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported 3 trees down along Highway 280 East.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed during afternoon/evening hours as a descending synoptic scale front and an inland progressing sea breeze enhanced moisture convergence over Georgia. Thunderstorms continued into early night hours as they interacted with meso-scale boundaries from previous thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A state official reported a tree down and blocking both lanes of Springfield-Egypt Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level trough remained evident across the eastern U.S., immediately to the west of the East Coast while a strong subtropical ridge remained anchored across the central and southern Plains.  A residual convective outflow boundary was evident across east Georgia, with a notable afternoon seabreeze in the southeast part of the state.  Thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon across central Georgia and the northeast mountains.  Numerous outflow boundaries allowed the thunderstorms to continue to develop and propagate erratically across north and central Georgia well into the evening hours thanks to a continued hot, humid air mass in place.  By far on this day, the most notable concentration of convective activity was across the eastern portion of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toombs County 911 Center observed quarter-sized hail near Lyons.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toombs County 911 Center reported that a couple of trees and a road sign were down around Lyons.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upstream convective complex cold pooled over inland Georgia, then transformed to a boundary driven convective event during the early night hours. Thunderstorms became severe as they entered a moderately unstable atmosphere featuring mixed layer CAPE between 2000-3000 J/KG.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of long duration drought conditions, hot temperatures and low humidity values, allowed lightning to start the Terrell Mill Pond Wildfire in southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Forestry Commission reported that the Terrell Mill Pond Wildfire, located 2 miles west of Hinesville, Georgia, was started due to a lightning strike on June 28, 2011 and continued to burn until it was contained on July 13, 2011.  The fire burned approximately 372 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of hot temperatures and high levels of humidity, resulted in heat indices which met or surpassed Excessive Heat Warning criteria across portions of southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heat index of 117 degrees was observed at the AWOS on the Hunter Army Airfield, 4 miles west-southwest of Savannah, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level trough remained evident across the eastern U.S., immediately to the west of the East Coast while a strong subtropical ridge remained anchored across the central and southern Plains.  A residual convective outflow boundary was evident across east Georgia, with a notable afternoon seabreeze in the southeast part of the state.  Thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon across central Georgia and the northeast mountains.  Numerous outflow boundaries allowed the thunderstorms to continue to develop and propagate erratically across north and central Georgia well into the evening hours thanks to a continued hot, humid air mass in place.  By far on this day, the most notable concentration of convective activity was across the eastern portion of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Barrow County 911 Center reported that several trees and a few power lines were down in the western part of the county between Winder and the Gwinnett county border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp and deep negatively tilted full-latitude upper trough was sweeping through the eastern half of the United States.  An unseasonably warm, moist air mass was present in advance of the front, as was the case with many events during the 2011 - 2012 winter season.  In addition, a strong low-level jet contributed highly to a high shear/high helicity environment and the development of rotating cells within a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) that tracked from west central to east central Georgia during the afternoon.  An EF2 tornado tracked from Lee county in east central Alabama into Harris county in west central Georgia.  In addition, there were several reports of quarter-sized hail and thunderstorm wind damage, primarily across central Georgia during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cobb County 911 Center reported that several trees, at least six, were down across the east central part of the county near Smyrna and Marietta.  Power outages were also noted in the Powder Springs area in the southwest part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed over the Blue Ridge and slowly drifted south into northeast Georgia. Large hail was reported with one of the storms.
Onshore flow along with elevated swell waves resulted in numerous rip currents along the Georgia coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguards reported 16 rip current related rescues were necessary at the Tybee Island, Georgia beaches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant late summer upper short wave was diving southeast from the mid-Mississippi Valley toward the southeastern United States.  A convective outflow boundary from overnight convective activity was present across central Georgia.  With ample moisture and continued hot temperatures, scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed quickly during the early and mid-afternoon across central Georgia, roughly from La Grange, to Thomaston, to Augusta southward.  A few isolated stronger thunderstorms were also noted across northeast Georgia during the afternoon.  Several of these thunderstorms became strong to severe producing damaging downburst winds and nickel to quarter-sized hail.  This was probably the most significant severe convective day for the month of August within the WFO FFC county warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained storm spotter observed several trees and power lines down between Hamilton and Waverly Hall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong line of severe thunderstorms moved through the CSRA and produced a lot of wind damage, mainly trees being uprooted or snapped off and powerlines being taken down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported trees down along the Thompson Bridge road area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved into the region from the north, spurring scattered thunderstorm development across southern South Carolina. The sea breeze then pushed inland and interacted with the storms, resulting in additional storm development and cell merges that meandered southwest into eastern Georgia before dissipating in the early evening hours. DCAPE values were between 1000-1100 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported nickel size hail in Springfield, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early Fall, seasonably strong upper level mid-latitude trough moved through the area on November 16th. The associated surface low pressure and cold front brought with it a round of thunderstorms, some of which became severe in southwest and south-central Georgia.  A tornado watch was in affect prior to the arrival of thunderstorms. However, no tornadoes were confirmed in the WFO Tallahassee county warning area with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Whigham Road.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early Fall, seasonably strong upper level mid-latitude trough moved through the area on November 16th. A tornado watch was in affect prior to the arrival of thunderstorms. However, no tornadoes were confirmed in the WFO Tallahassee county warning area with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off a barn along Bolling Road.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp and deep negatively tilted full-latitude upper trough was sweeping through the eastern half of the United States.  An unseasonably warm, moist air mass was present in advance of the front, as was the case with many events during the 2011 - 2012 winter season.  In addition, a strong low-level jet contributed highly to a high shear/high helicity environment and the development of rotating cells within a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) that tracked from west central to east central Georgia during the afternoon.  An EF2 tornado tracked from Lee county in east central Alabama into Harris county in west central Georgia.  In addition, there were several reports of quarter-sized hail and thunderstorm wind damage, primarily across central Georgia during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toombs County 911 Center reported that three trees were down near Lyons, one of which was down on a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant late summer upper short wave was diving southeast from the mid-Mississippi Valley toward the southeastern United States.  A convective outflow boundary from overnight convective activity was present across central Georgia.  With ample moisture and continued hot temperatures, scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed quickly during the early and mid-afternoon across central Georgia, roughly from La Grange, to Thomaston, to Augusta southward.  A few isolated stronger thunderstorms were also noted across northeast Georgia during the afternoon.  Several of these thunderstorms became strong to severe producing damaging downburst winds and nickel to quarter-sized hail.  This was probably the most significant severe convective day for the month of August within the WFO FFC county warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jones County 911 Center reported that a couple of power lines and a couple of trees were down mainly across the southwest half of the county near the Bibb and Twiggs county lines.  WSR-88D base velocity data indicated that the strongest winds moved through this area of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upstream convective complex cold pooled over inland Georgia, then transformed to a boundary driven convective event during the early night hours. Thunderstorms became severe as they entered a moderately unstable atmosphere featuring mixed layer CAPE between 2000-3000 J/KG.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 1 large pine tree down along with numerous branches and power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line moved across southeast Georgia around 8 pm local time. Widespread wind damage occurred from Coffee county to Ware county in isolated severe cells. An EF1 tornado produced damage about 5 miles SSE of Dixie Union in Ware County. The previous day, record high temperatures were set across north Florida due to strong warm air advection ahead of the front. A strong low level jet moved across the area overnight behind the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county 911 dispatch center reported that roofs were torn off of sheds and a trampoline was blown into the middle of the road. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms developed near a long-wave trough east of Georgia. Thunderstorms became strong as they entered an unstable environment featuring MLCAPE between 2000-2500 J/KG and DCAPE over 1000 J/KG. The sea breeze and outflow boundaries enhanced deep moist updrafts and supported multicell thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong near 600 dm subtropical ridge remained across Texas and the southern Plains.  Unstable northwest flow aloft along the eastern periphery of this upper high continued across the Mid-Atlantic and southeast U.S.  A quasi-stationary front was located from west to east across north Georgia.  A disturbance in the northwest flow aloft was approaching and would push the front further south in the coming days.  These features combined with a hot, unstable air mass to support isolated strong to severe thunderstorm development across mainly north Georgia.  Thunderstorms developed and collapsed quickly, yet the environment on this day was particularly conducive to wet microbursts.  Widespread downed trees were noted in Fayette, Gwinnett, Henry, and Barrow counties from brief, yet intense thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fayette County Emergency Management Director reported that at least a dozen trees were blown down just east of Tyrone and near Sandy Creek in the area of Tyrone Road, Dogwood Trail, Adams Road, and Sandy Creek Road.  Several power lines were also brought down by the downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong subtropical upper ridge over the southern plains remained in place.  However, the eastern extremity of the upper ridge across the mid-south was beginning to weaken as a short wave moved southeast through the Ohio and into the Eastern Tennessee Valley.  A moist, hot, unstable air mass remained in place across the southeast U.S.  The more favorable synoptic environment allowed for increased coverage of diurnal showers and thunderstorm activity.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed form most of these storms.  Isolated storms in west central and central Georgia became severe and produced quarter to half-dollar-sized hail during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed quarter to slightly less than golf ball-sized hail across far north central Muscogee county.  Most of the hail was the size of quarters to half-dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed in the northeast Gulf of Mexico and tracked eastward across the northern Florida peninsula, then northeastward off the southeast Georgia and southern South Carolina coast.  Meanwhile, a shallow cold airmass remained in place in the lee of the Appalachians by high pressure north of the area, allowing a continued supply of cold and dry air at the surface.  The warm temperatures well above ground level and freezing or sub-freezing temperatures at ground level, resulted in freezing rain and ice accumulation across much of southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported a tree down due to ice accumulation on Highway 301 near the county airport in Claxton, Georgia.  Ice accumulation was also estimated to be around 0.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the Monday morning of January 10, 2011, freezing rain fell across portions of southern Georgia and southeast Alabama. Amounts generally ranged from a trace to a quarter of an inch, but a few areas had half an inch of total ice accumulation. In southern Georgia, the main impacts from the ice storm were hazardous road conditions and power outages. In southeast Alabama, where there was less ice, the impact was limited to slippery roads. This was a very rare event for the region, and warning criteria ice accretion at the time was defined locally as any measurable ice accretion of greater than a trace. Ice accretion reports first started to come in around 5 am EST /4 am CST/ with freezing rain ending around 9 am EST /8 am CST/. The highest amount reported was in Terrell County, GA with around a half inch of accretion. The counties of Turner, Worth, Randolph, and Lee in Georgia reported around a quarter inch of accretion. The rest of the counties reported less than a quarter inch of accretion. Many schools were closed for the day on Monday. The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate that only includes the Georgia counties.  For the estimate on the Alabama counties, please see that entry.

A deep upper low continued to meander about the central Appalachians and the mid-Atlantic region.  A series of weak disturbances continued to rotate around the upper low across the southeast U.S.  While across most of north and central Georgia, the atmosphere had become too cool and stable to support much thunderstorm activity, more unstable, moist air remained present across the far southeast part of the county warning area.  This supported afternoon and evening thunderstorm development once again.  A few of these thunderstorms became strong and produced penny to nickel-sized hail, but none of the thunderstorms reached severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Macon television media relayed reports of nickel-sized hail around Wrens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions contributed to an increased risk of wildfires during November.  While most of the wildfires occurred in the northeast Georgia area, one wildfire affected an area slightly over five acres in Putnam county on November 9th.  The cause of the fire was indicated to be lightning, however no lightning was observed in the area at the time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The United States Forecast Service confirmed that a wildfire, labeled Martin Mill, affected a 5.3 acre area of Putnam county.  The cause of the fire was indicated to be lightning, although no thunderstorm activity was observed in the area at the time.  Damages related to the fire were estimated at $1,500.

A strong short wave, embedded within a fast zonal flow, impinged on an unseasonably warm, moist air mass for late November supporting scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms.  The activity was largely linear organized and indicative of typical quasi-linear convective systems.  An apparent microburst on the leading edge of a line of thunderstorms in central Georgia resulted in the destruction of a chicken house in southern Houston county.  No other damage reports were received from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houston County Emergency Management Director reported that an apparent microburst along the leading edge of a line of thunderstorms completely destroyed a chicken house.  The damage was extremely localized as none of the neighboring structures suffered any damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with a vigorous upper level trough crossed the region during the day. In response to the tight pressure gradient, strong winds developed out ahead of the front as well as in the cold advection behind it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down across Chatham County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the Monday morning of January 10, 2011, freezing rain fell across portions of southern Georgia and southeast Alabama. Amounts generally ranged from a trace to a quarter of an inch, but a few areas had half an inch of total ice accumulation. In southern Georgia, the main impacts from the ice storm were hazardous road conditions and power outages. In southeast Alabama, where there was less ice, the impact was limited to slippery roads. This was a very rare event for the region, and warning criteria ice accretion at the time was defined locally as any measurable ice accretion of greater than a trace. Ice accretion reports first started to come in around 5 am EST /4 am CST/ with freezing rain ending around 9 am EST /8 am CST/. The highest amount reported was in Terrell County, GA with around a half inch of accretion. The counties of Turner, Worth, Randolph, and Lee in Georgia reported around a quarter inch of accretion. The rest of the counties reported less than a quarter inch of accretion. Many schools were closed for the day on Monday. The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate that only includes the Georgia counties.  For the estimate on the Alabama counties, please see that entry.

An area of low pressure developed in the northeast Gulf of Mexico and tracked eastward across the northern Florida peninsula, then northeastward off the southeast Georgia and southern South Carolina coast.  Meanwhile, a shallow cold airmass remained in place in the lee of the Appalachians by high pressure north of the area, allowing a continued supply of cold and dry air at the surface.  The warm temperatures well above ground level and freezing or sub-freezing temperatures at ground level, resulted in freezing rain and ice accumulation across much of southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Candler County Warning Point reported tree limbs and power lines down due to ice accumulation across the county.

A broad upper level trough located west of the area provided southwesterly flow and a series of upper level disturbances ejecting northeastward.  Moist southerly low level flow off the Gulf of Mexico provided ample moisture.  The result was pockets of heavy rain in excess of 3 inches in a 24 hour period across portions of southeast Alabama, southwest Georgia, and northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rainfall total of 3.05 inches was measured by the USGS river gauge located at Pataula Creek near the intersection of US Highway 82 and County Road 46.

Moderate to heavy snow associated with a Gulf Coast storm system developed over the Upper Savannah River Valley during the late evening, and quickly accumulated. By 4 AM, most areas had around 4 inches of snow. The snow became lighter prior to sunrise, but continued to accumulate through the morning. By early afternoon, precipitation changed to light freezing rain and, and eventually to rain as temperatures warmed above freezing. This added a thin layer of ice to the 4 to 8 inches of snow that fell across northeast Georgia. Persistent cold temperatures resulted in several cycles of melting and refreezing, and some schools and businesses remained closed for several days.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported quarter size hail between Cobbtown, Georgia and Collins, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary in combination with modest instability and strong deep layer shear, resulted in scattered strong to severe thunderstorm development across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported nickel size hail covering the ground in Springfield, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous short wave was moving rapidly from the south central to the southeast United States, accompanied by a strong Pacific cold front.  Unseasonably warm air, which had prevailed across the southeast U.S. much of the later half of February, combined with strong shear, to support a strong to severe quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) that tracked slowly southeast across the state from mid-afternoon through early evening.  Several tornado warnings were issued, but no tornadoes were reported with this event.  However, several counties across north Georgia received damaging winds and quarter to half-dollars-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartow County Emergency Management Director reported that many trees were down in the Adairsville area of northern Bartow county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gordon County Emergency Management Director reported that a few power lines were down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low was moving through the northeast United States, while a semi-permanent upper low was located across the southwest United States.  Arctic air enveloped much of the central and southern Plains.  A strong cold front, associated with the northeast U.S. upper low, was sweeping through the southeast U.S.  A large area of showers and thunderstorms accompanied the front.  While none of the thunderstorms became severe, frequent lightning was observed in the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County Emergency Managment Director reported that lightning struck a large oak tree adjacent to a residence on Mintons Chapel Road near Kite.  The adjacent structure did not suffer any damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Progressive zonal flow had dominated the southern United States since mid-February.  Temperatures were unseasonably warm across the area and moisture levels near to above seasonal normals.  A vigorous short wave, embedded within the strong westerly flow, and associated strong Pacific cold front quickly traversed the area during the early morning hours of the 25th.  A significant low-level jet and strong low-level shear were noted with this system contributing to strong low-level linear forcing.  Thus, synoptic conditions were favorable for a narrow line of showers and thunderstorms, or quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) to quickly traverse the area during the early morning hours.  Most of the thunderstorms were marginally severe blowing down a few trees.  Some of the trees also landed on and brought down power lines.  The event produced no hail and no tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Catoosa County 911 Center reported that three trees were down, also knocking down three power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous short wave was moving rapidly from the south central to the southeast United States, accompanied by a strong Pacific cold front.  Unseasonably warm air, which had prevailed across the southeast U.S. much of the later half of February, combined with strong shear, to support a strong to severe quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) that tracked slowly southeast across the state from mid-afternoon through early evening.  Several tornado warnings were issued, but no tornadoes were reported with this event.  However, several counties across north Georgia received damaging winds and quarter to half-dollars-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gordon County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck an abandoned trailer.  Specific details regarding damages were not provided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough and closed low were moving from the south central U.S. toward the mid-Atlantic region.  This system brought widespread moderate to heavy rain to north and central Georgia.  Most areas of the state received two to three inches of rain from this event.  A massive area of Arctic air covered much of the United States east of the Rockies.  However, an upper ridge located across south Florida and Bermuda was helping to steer the majority of this Arctic air just northeast of Georgia.  Nonetheless, the cold air was deep enough initially across north Georgia for the precipitation to begin as a mix of snow and sleet, before changing to all rain by daybreak on the 4th.  Areas north of a Carrollton, to Cumming, to Cleveland line saw on average 1/2 inch of snow.  The greatest amount of snow was reported from Chattooga county with 1.0 inch to 1.5 inches of snow.  Some sleet and isolated pockets of freezing rain were also noted at the beginning of the event.  Since surface and ground temperatures generally held in the 32 to 34F range, no significant travel problems were noted with this event.  Below is a listing of winter weather reports received from north Georgia counties.||Carroll county - a trace of sleet, and a trace of ice accumulation.  Some ice noted on trees around Carrollton;|Chattooga county - 1.0 to 1.5 inches of snow;|Cherokee county - 0.5 inch of snow;|Dawson county - < 0.5 inch of snow;|Douglas county - 0.1 inch of ice accumulation;|Floyd county - 0.5 inch of snow;|Forsyth county - 0.5 inch of snow;|Paulding county - a trace of snow;|Polk county - < 0.5 inch of snow;|Union county - 0.5 inch of snow and 0.1 inch of ice accumulation in Blairsville;|White county - 0.5 inch of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga County Emergency Management Director reported from 1.0 to 1.5 inches of snow across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous short wave was moving rapidly from the south central to the southeast United States, accompanied by a strong Pacific cold front.  Unseasonably warm air, which had prevailed across the southeast U.S. much of the later half of February, combined with strong shear, to support a strong to severe quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) that tracked slowly southeast across the state from mid-afternoon through early evening.  Several tornado warnings were issued, but no tornadoes were reported with this event.  However, several counties across north Georgia received damaging winds and quarter to half-dollars-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Madison County 911 Center reported that more than a dozen trees and at least three power lines were down throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough and closed low were moving from the south central U.S. toward the mid-Atlantic region.  This system brought widespread moderate to heavy rain to north and central Georgia.  Most areas of the state received two to three inches of rain from this event.  A massive area of Arctic air covered much of the United States east of the Rockies.  However, an upper ridge located across south Florida and Bermuda was helping to steer the majority of this Arctic air just northeast of Georgia.  Nonetheless, the cold air was deep enough initially across north Georgia for the precipitation to begin as a mix of snow and sleet, before changing to all rain by daybreak on the 4th.  Areas north of a Carrollton, to Cumming, to Cleveland line saw on average 1/2 inch of snow.  The greatest amount of snow was reported from Chattooga county with 1.0 inch to 1.5 inches of snow.  Some sleet and isolated pockets of freezing rain were also noted at the beginning of the event.  Since surface and ground temperatures generally held in the 32 to 34F range, no significant travel problems were noted with this event.  Below is a listing of winter weather reports received from north Georgia counties.||Carroll county - a trace of sleet, and a trace of ice accumulation.  Some ice noted on trees around Carrollton;|Chattooga county - 1.0 to 1.5 inches of snow;|Cherokee county - 0.5 inch of snow;|Dawson county - < 0.5 inch of snow;|Douglas county - 0.1 inch of ice accumulation;|Floyd county - 0.5 inch of snow;|Forsyth county - 0.5 inch of snow;|Paulding county - a trace of snow;|Polk county - < 0.5 inch of snow;|Union county - 0.5 inch of snow and 0.1 inch of ice accumulation in Blairsville;|White county - 0.5 inch of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Carroll County Emergency Management Director reported a trace of sleet and a trace of ice accumulation on the trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A briefed tornado touchdown occurred near Interstate 95 at mile post 42. There was no damage. The tornado lifted after making brief ground contact.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The leading edge of a pre-frontal squall line started to impact southeast Georgia and the Suwannee River Valley of north Florida prior to sunrise. Widespread wind damage was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike on April 28th ignited a wildfire in the Okefenokee National Wildlife Refuge in the section of the park known as Honey Prairie. Prolonged drought conditions and sea breeze regimes helped the fire spread across much of the swamp during the month of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Honey Prairie Wildfire complex engulfed much of the Okefenokee Swamp during the month of May 2011. On May 1st, the fire burned about 750 acres. By the evening of May 6th, the fire had burned about 29,852 acres. On May 10th, the fire burned 19,521 acres in 24 hrs, which damaged a total of 81,434 acres. On the 18th, the fire had burned 146,924 acres and was 55 percent contained. Stephen Foster State Park and Swamp Island Drive at the Okefenokee Swamp East Entrance were closed on the 18th. By the 20th, the fire was 66 percent contained. By the 23rd, the fire had consumed 148,424 acres and was 70 percent contained. On the 28th the fire remained 70 percent contained and has burned 149,550 acres.  Stephen Foster State Park remained closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line moved across southeast Georgia during the late afternoon and early evening hours where a strong low level jet phased under a lifting upper level trough; both downstream of the approaching surface cold front. A trailing band of convection moved across northeast Florida during the latter half of the evening when the surface front phased with the trailing lobe of positive vorticity advection under the lifting upper level trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall with possible funnel clouds were reported along Interstate 95. Wind gusts were estimated to 50 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county sheriff relayed that they had received numerous reports of trees and power line damage due to thunderstorm winds across Hazlehurst city limits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough and closed low were moving from the south central U.S. toward the mid-Atlantic region.  This system brought widespread moderate to heavy rain to north and central Georgia.  Most areas of the state received two to three inches of rain from this event.  A massive area of Arctic air covered much of the United States east of the Rockies.  However, an upper ridge located across south Florida and Bermuda was helping to steer the majority of this Arctic air just northeast of Georgia.  Nonetheless, the cold air was deep enough initially across north Georgia for the precipitation to begin as a mix of snow and sleet, before changing to all rain by daybreak on the 4th.  Areas north of a Carrollton, to Cumming, to Cleveland line saw on average 1/2 inch of snow.  The greatest amount of snow was reported from Chattooga county with 1.0 inch to 1.5 inches of snow.  Some sleet and isolated pockets of freezing rain were also noted at the beginning of the event.  Since surface and ground temperatures generally held in the 32 to 34F range, no significant travel problems were noted with this event.  Below is a listing of winter weather reports received from north Georgia counties.||Carroll county - a trace of sleet, and a trace of ice accumulation.  Some ice noted on trees around Carrollton;|Chattooga county - 1.0 to 1.5 inches of snow;|Cherokee county - 0.5 inch of snow;|Dawson county - < 0.5 inch of snow;|Douglas county - 0.1 inch of ice accumulation;|Floyd county - 0.5 inch of snow;|Forsyth county - 0.5 inch of snow;|Paulding county - a trace of snow;|Polk county - < 0.5 inch of snow;|Union county - 0.5 inch of snow and 0.1 inch of ice accumulation in Blairsville;|White county - 0.5 inch of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Union County 911 Center reported 0.5 inch of snow throughout much of the county, and up to 0.1 inch of ice accumulation in the Blairsville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stacked troughing moved across the area during the afternoon when instability was high as the sea breezes fronts began to progress inland. Numerous severe storms developed with hail the main convective hazard due to cold 500 mb temps in the -10 to -12 degree Celsius range and large CAPE and adequate shear in the hail growth zone. Gusty winds occurred, but they were mostly associated with collapsing storms. Low level 850 mb winds were only 20 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Satilla Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous short wave was moving rapidly from the south central to the southeast United States, accompanied by a strong Pacific cold front.  Unseasonably warm air, which had prevailed across the southeast U.S. much of the later half of February, combined with strong shear, to support a strong to severe quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) that tracked slowly southeast across the state from mid-afternoon through early evening.  Several tornado warnings were issued, but no tornadoes were reported with this event.  However, several counties across north Georgia received damaging winds and quarter to half-dollars-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker County Emergency Management Director reported that over a dozen trees were down between Chickamauga and Rock Spring.  Power lines were also down in the Rossville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad and highly diffluent upper trough was moving slowly through the south central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A pattern that became all too familiar across the U.S. during the later half of March and much of April.  As the trough moved into the eastern U.S. during the 10th and 11th, it deepened significantly and took on a strong negative title across the eastern U.S.  This system was associated with widespread severe weather and flooding across the Arklatex region, the lower Mississippi Valley, and the western Tennessee Valley region.  As the system spread into Georgia, it also produced flooding, flash flooding, and severe weather.  Widespread rain and thunderstorms spread across northwest Georgia late on the 8th and continued into the early morning hours of the 9th.  This precipitation was culminated by a near solid north-south oriented squall line or quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) that tracked across north and central Georgia.  The QLCS entered west Georgia around 9 am and tracked into east Georgia during the early afternoon.  Moderate to heavy rain continued behind this QLCS into the afternoon across northwest Georgia, finally ending by evening.  Rainfall of four to five inches was common across northwest Georgia from LaFayette, to Calhoun, to Cedartown, and Buchanan.  Several flash flood and flood events were observed in this part of the state.||As the squall line or QLCS moved into central and east Georgia it began to form bowing segments and line-echo-wave patterns (LEWPs) resulting in several damaging wind events across this part of the state.  Fortunately, no tornadoes were observed with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Treutlen County Emergency Management Director reported that a home in Soperton sustained minor damaged from straight-line thunderstorm winds.  In addition, three trees were blown down near Lothair in the western part of the county.

A total of 15 homes across the county were damaged from downed trees, including two with major damage and four others with moderate damage.  A middle-aged female was injured at one of the badly damaged homes when a large pine tree fell on her home.  Amateur radio operators reported that the back wall of a building on Cherokee Drive in Ball Ground was blown down.  Widespread power outages were also observed, especially in the Woodstock area.

The second area of damage was in the Trenton area where widespread downed trees were observed.  A few power lines were also down in this area as well.  Overall, hundreds of trees were down across the northern half of the county.
